Usability Test

Participants

These designs were usability tested with seven console gamers in a usability lab at the Microsoft Corporation. The gamers ranged in age from 16–26 and varied from 1–15 hours per week in time spent playing console games.

Apparatus

Participants used a prototype Xbox controller to provide input during this usability test. The controller was the same shape as an Xbox controller, but it had been programmed to output PC-readable data and was connected to the PC via a USB port. Participants viewed the prototypes on a 25-inch Sony Trinitron television that was showing an NTSC-encoded computer video signal at 640 x 480 pixel resolution.

Method

Participants completed 14 tasks saving games and loading games using 12 different Macromedia Director prototypes. There were 6 prototypes for load games and 6 for save games. Each participant used the prototypes in a different order. After completing each task, participants rated it on ease of use, time to complete, and the support messages. The scales came from the After Scenario Questionnaire (ASQ), Lewis, 19951. We also had them complete the Post System Study Usability Questionnaire (PSSUQ), which consists of 19 items and breaks usability of the product down into four factors: overall usability, system usefulness, information quality, and interface quality.

1 Lewis, J.R. (1995). IBM Computer Usability Satisfaction Questionnaires: Psychometric Evaluation and Instructions for Use, International Journal of Human-Computer Interaction, 7(1), 57-78..

Unpublished work. © 2000 Microsoft Corporation. All rights reserved.
